result:
Let’s talk about silence—not the absence of sound, but the presence of something so large that sound simply has no room. I grew up in a house where silence was a third parent. It stood at the stove next to my mother, stirring the soup with an invisible wooden spoon. It sat in the cracked leather recliner that smelled of my father’s aftershave and stacks of unread newspapers, breathing with the slow metronome of his chest. It followed me down the hallway when I tried to tiptoe past my older brother’s room, a hallway so narrow the walls sweated in summer and bled frost in winter. Silence had weight; it pressed on my sternum like the flat of a cold iron. If I spoke too loudly, I felt I might dent it, and then what would come rushing in? Everything we’d agreed not to say.

I used to think silence was the same as peace. I know better now. Peace is an orchard at dawn when the fruit is still holding the night’s coolness. Silence is the orchard after the locusts, the fruit gnawed hollow, the leaves brittle tickets to a show that closed last week. And yet I still love it. I court it. I wake at 4:00 a.m. when even the highway half a mile away has grown tired of its own voice, and I sit on the back step with a chipped mug of coffee, listening to the silence rearrange itself around my breathing. Sometimes I imagine it as a large cat, black fur absorbing starlight, blinking once to acknowledge me before it returns to its vigil. I do not pet it. I do not speak. I simply let it decide whether I am predator, prey, or furniture.

When I was eight, I tried to bury silence in the backyard. I thought if I could dig a hole deep enough, we could drop it in like a penny in a well and wish for something louder. I used the rusted shovel my grandfather left behind, the one with the split handle that pinched your palm if you held it wrong. The ground was October-hard, argumentative. Each shovelful came up clenched, full of pebbles that looked like baby teeth. I sang while I dug—mostly nonsense, “Row, Row, Row Your Boat” stretched like taffy—because I believed noise could gag silence. Instead, the song sank. The hole got deeper, and the silence leaned over my shoulder, curious, the way adults watch children do cartwheels: benevolent, amused, certain the stunt will end in scraped knees.

I never finished. Twilight climbed out of the trees and wrapped itself around my wrists, and I understood that darkness is just silence wearing a different coat. I left the shovel standing upright like a knight who’d lost interest in the grail. The next morning the hole was filled with leaves, but not the kind that fall. These were green, torn off by wind, still holding the shape of the branch they’d left. It looked as if the ground had grown a tongue. I covered it with the blue plastic wading pool we never used anymore because my brother said pools were for babies. The water had evaporated, leaving a ghost line of calcium like ring-around-the-collar. I figured if silence wouldn’t stay buried, at least I could give it a skylight.

Years later, in a city far enough away that the accents sounded like someone slowly turning the radio dial, I discovered that silence could be rented by the hour. The university library had a sixth floor designed by a misanthrope: concrete walls the color of dishwater, lights that hummed in a frequency only teenagers and dogs can hear, carrels the size of confessionals. You signed your name on a clipboard and were granted a cubicle with an outlet and just enough square footage to lose your religion in. I went there when my dorm room became a parade of guitars, egos, and the persistent smell of ramen seasoning packets. I’d close the little plywood door, rest my forehead on the cool fake-wood laminate, and wait for the silence to show up. Sometimes it arrived wearing courier’s clothes, hand-delivering memories I hadn’t ordered: my mother’s lipstick growing old in the bottom of a purse, the way my brother used to clip his nails into a cereal bowl, the last conversation with my father where the words “I’m proud of you” got jammed in the pay-phone slot and we both pretended the line had gone dead.

I wrote papers about Shakespeare’s fools and Marx’s commodities, but in the margins I drafted instructions for surviving the quiet:

1. Do not breathe too loudly; it will think you are singing and try to harmonize.
2. If your heart begins to sound like footsteps in an empty auditorium, place two fingers on your pulse and whisper, “I am the janitor, I have keys, this is not an intruder.”
3. When you remember that every promise you ever made is archived somewhere inside your cells, let the tears come. Salt is the only language silence understands; it will lick your cheeks like a deer at a salt lick, then retreat.

Eventually I learned you can’t pay silence to stay. One night the fluorescent lights blinked twice and went dark, not a power outage, just the library’s way of saying Go home, you’ve milked the cosmos long enough. I walked down six flights of stairs, each step echoing like a slow handclap for a departing guest, and stepped into a snow that had started while I was inside. The city had been erased by a white eraser, leaving only the faint indentation of what had been. My footprints sounded like someone tearing paper. I realized then that silence and snow are cousins; both arrive when the world grows tired of its own adjectives.

I kept chasing it. After college I took a job teaching English in a town built on the lip of a canyon. The map said “population 312,” but I counted 17 dogs, 2,000 piñon trees, and an indeterminate number of ancestors who refused to leave just because their bodies had. The school was a double-wide trailer backed up against red cliffs that sweated iron ore. My students were Navajo and Hopi kids who spoke in the cadence of far-off thunder, English vowels stretched like taffy over canyon rims. They taught me that silence can be a verb. “We’re silencing now,” they’d say when the lesson ended and we sat watching crows argue over whose turn it was to drive the thermals. They meant: we are giving the world back its original tongue.

One boy, Leland, stayed after class to avoid the bus ride across the rez. His father was a drinking man who kept calendars from 1997 because, he claimed, those were the last years that fit right. Leland would draw superheroes on scrap paper, capes longer than the page, folding time so the future could catch up. We never talked much. He’d sit at the desk nearest the space heater that smelled of burnt camel hair, and I’d grade papers, the only sound the sigh of metal expanding. One December afternoon the sky spat out every flake it had been saving for a decade. School let out early, but Leland didn’t leave. We watched the snow erase the parking lot, the soccer goalposts becoming exclamation points written by someone who’d forgotten the sentence. After an hour he said, “You ever notice snow makes the quiet visible?” I told him about the library, the cubicle, the tears. He nodded like someone confirming directions. Then he opened his notebook and showed me a drawing: a figure wearing a cloak of silence, threads spun from negative space, hem stitched with unspoke words. Where the face should be, only a mirror. I looked into it and saw the hole I’d started digging when I was eight, still open, still green-tongued.

That spring the canyon caught fire. Lightning kissed a juniper, and the whole slope went up like a birthday candle on a cake nobody planned to eat. We evacuated the school, stood across the highway while flames performed autopsies on the piñons. The smoke smelled of incense and endings. No one spoke. Even the sirens felt embarrassed by their own noise. I glanced at Leland; he was sketching again, charcoal smudges blooming on his fingertips like dusk. Later I saw the picture: fire rendered only by the absence of color, white space doing all the burning. At the bottom he’d written: “Silence is what’s left when the world exhales and decides not to inhale again.”

I left the rez the next year. Not because of the fire—fires regenerate, everyone knows that—but because I realized you can’t inhabit someone else’s quiet without eventually paying rent in blood memory. I drove west until the ocean appeared like a blue garage door someone had forgotten to close. California smelled of eucalyptus and unkept promises. I got a job in a bookstore that had once been a funeral parlor; the self-help section lived in the former chapel, which meant people browsed grief recovery under stained-glass lilies. During breaks I’d sit on the loading dock and listen to the fog eat the sun. The boss played free-form jazz over the speakers, saxophones that sounded like they were apologizing for something that hadn’t happened yet. Customers complained, but I liked the way the notes collided with the high ceilings and fell back down like birds testing damaged wings. It proved that even aimless noise yearns for an echo.

One Thursday a woman came in asking for books on silence. She wore a coat the color of wet sand and carried a canvas bag embroidered with the words “Quiet Car, Amtrak 2013.” I walked her to the meditation section, but she waved it away like smoke. “Not how-to,” she said. “Poetry of. Cartography of.” I told her we had John Cage, but she already owned 4'33" on vinyl, CD, and a bootleg cassette recorded in a thunderstorm. We ended up in the basement, aisle 9B, where the remainders went to die. I pulled out a coffee-table book of photographs from the Hubble telescope: galaxies spun sugar, nebulae the color of bruised plums. She opened to a page showing the Boötes Void—250 million light-years of almost nothing. Her finger hovered above the image as if she might touch the absence and come away changed. “This,” she said, “is the silence I’m after. The kind that doesn’t know we exist.” She bought two copies, one to keep, one to give to her brother who’d stopped speaking after his third tour. At the register I asked why the Quiet Car. She said that once, somewhere outside Albany, the train lost power. For six minutes the only sound was the slow collapse of passengers’ expectations. She heard her own heart make a fist, then open. “I’ve been trying to get back there,” she admitted. “But you can’t buy a ticket to the past.” She left, and the automatic door sighed like a parent who’s said all there is to say.

That night I rode my bicycle to the pier. The fog had clocked out, leaving the sky so clear the stars looked like nail heads in velvet. I thought about the void she’d shown me, how it was already ancient news by the time its light arrived to be photographed. I tried to imagine a silence that pre-existed us, one that would continue shampooing the universe long after the last.concurrent.fall of the last.concussed empire. I couldn’t. My imagination kept slipping on the blood-pressure swoosh in my ears. So I did the only sensible thing: I jumped. Not to die—just to feel the splash. The Pacific was cold enough to reboot every memory I’d ever had. Underwater, sound travels four times faster; the quiet inside my head became a cathedral packed with whales confessing to one another in ultrasonic baritone. I surfaced laughing, which made a small hole in the ocean, quickly filled.

I’ve stopped trying to bury or rent silence. These days we coexist like roommates who split utilities but not secrets. I live in a studio apartment above a laundromat; the machines thump like sneakers in a dryer, even when they’re off. At night I open the window and listen to the freeway add exclamation points to the dark. Somewhere down there a motorcycle believes it’s a comet. I don’t mind. I’ve learned to treat urban noise as the negative space that lets silence pop like a photo negative. I write on the walls with dry-erase markers—quotes I half remember, grocery lists, apologies to people I’ll never see again. Every month I wipe them clean, enjoying the squeak of felt against paint, the way it sounds like a slate being cleared by a teacher who’s decided the lesson is hopeless but the day must go on.

Last week I drove back to the orchard town of my childhood. The house had been vinyl-sided the color of cafeteria peas. My old backyard had a trampoline now, safety net like a parishioner’s veil. Kids I couldn’t name were launching themselves skyward, achieving the brief weightlessness we all insist on calling fun. I parked across the street, engine running, AC sighing like an unpaid therapist. The hole I’d started was still there, hidden under years of lawn clippings and thawed winters. No wading pool, no salt lick deer. Just grass lying the way hair lies over a healing scar. I thought about knocking, offering the current owners a percentage of my memories, but they were busy being young inside the hush of central air. So I left the engine in idle and rolled down the window. Across the road, the orchard had been subdivided into cul-de-sacs with names like Whispering Pine and Covenant Creek, though the only water was a fountain at the roundabout depicting a boy catching a fish that would never grow larger than the lie he’d tell about it.

I waited for the trampoline kids to stop jumping, to collapse into the grass and let the silence arrive like a traveling carnival that sets up overnight and is gone before the authorities can inspect the rides. It didn’t come. Instead, a dog barked two streets away, setting off another dog, and another, a relay race of complaint that circled the town twice and returned panting to its owner. I realized then that silence had moved out long ago, probably my freshman year, taking only what it could carry: one ceramic bowl chipped at the rim, a single sock, the smell of my mother’s lipstick when the tube rolled under the seat and melted. It left no forwarding address, just a note: “You’ll find me where you stop looking.” I put the car in drive and headed west again, sun at my back like a lighthouse bored of its own cautionary tale.

Tonight, back in the laundromat apartment, I’ve turned off every appliance. The refrigerator hums in protest, but I unplugged it, knowing the milk will forgive me by morning. I sit on the floorboards and watch a spider lower itself from the ceiling, spinning a thread so fine it could be the period at the end of this sentence. Somewhere inside the wall, mice are discussing real estate. Outside, the city exhales hot asphalt breath. I wait. Not for revelation, not for forgiveness—just for the moment when the air realizes it’s being watched and forgets how to move. It takes a while. There’s always leakage: the distant doppler of a siren, the elevator in my chest arriving at the cardiac floor, the memory of my father saying my name as if it were a question he regretted asking. I catalog each intruder the way astronomers log near-miss asteroids: noted, filed, released.

And then, without announcement, it arrives. Not the library hush, not the snow hush, not the void that ate the galaxies. Just this room, these floorboards, this heart learning again how to be a fist and then unbecome. The spider touches down on my knee, thinks better of it, rappels elsewhere. The mice declare bankruptcy. I become the hole I started digging at eight, only now it’s upside-down, a chimney to somewhere. Words rise like warm air: I’m sorry. Thank you. Please. They climb until they cool and drift back as the dust I’ll sweep tomorrow. I understand finally that silence isn’t an absence, isn’t a presence, isn’t even a roommate. It’s the permission slip we sign for ourselves, the one that reads, “You may leave the party early, no excuse needed.” I sign with the pen of no ink. The paper is the room. The signature is the way the walls, accustomed to keeping the world out, briefly lean in to listen.

I could keep writing—about the woman who bought the star atlas and mailed me a postcard from the Quiet Car saying only, “Still here,” or about Leland who now teaches art at the community college and sends photos of his students’ sculptures: a ladder made entirely of hangers, a dress sewn from unmailed letters. I could describe the orchard’s last apple tree, moved to a museum courtyard, fruiting every other year despite the exhaust. But 2500 words is a fence, and fences are just suggestions to silence, which recognizes no deed, no surveyor, no title insurance. So I’ll stop here, mid-breath, and let the hush finish the story the way night finishes day—by not finishing at all, only folding the light and slipping it into the pocket of whatever comes next.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
